Math401Math 401, Notes 3

Coding and Information Theory Crash Course

Encoding

Let A,BA,BA,B be two finite sets with size a,ba,ba,b respectively.

Let S(A)=⋃r=1∞ArS(A)=\bigcup_{r=1}^{\infty}A^rS(A)=⋃r=1∞​Ar be the word semigroup generated by AAA.

A one-to-one mapping f:A→S(B)f:A\to S(B)f:A→S(B) is called a code with message alphabet AAA and encoded alphabet BBB.

Example:

  • A=A=A= RGB color space
  • B={0∼255}B=\{0\sim 255\}B={0∼255}
  • f:A→Bnf:A\to B^nf:A→Bn is a code

For example, f(white)=(255,255,255)f(white)=(255,255,255)f(white)=(255,255,255), f(green)=(0,255,0)f(green)=(0,255,0)f(green)=(0,255,0)

Uniquely decipherable codes

A code f:A→S(B)f:A\to S(B)f:A→S(B) is called uniquely decipherable if the extension code

f~:S(A)→S(B)=f(a1)f(a2)⋯f(an)\tilde{f}:S(A)\to S(B)=f(a_1)f(a_2)\cdots f(a_n)f~​:S(A)→S(B)=f(a1​)f(a2​)⋯f(an​)

is one-to-one.

Example:

  • A={a,b,c,d}A=\{a,b,c,d\}A={a,b,c,d}
  • B={0,1}B=\{0,1\}B={0,1}
  • f(a)=00f(a)=00f(a)=00, f(b)=01f(b)=01f(b)=01, f(c)=10f(c)=10f(c)=10, f(d)=11f(d)=11f(d)=11

is uniquely decipherable.

  • f(a)=0f(a)=0f(a)=0, f(b)=1f(b)=1f(b)=1, f(c)=10f(c)=10f(c)=10, f(d)=11f(d)=11f(d)=11

is not uniquely decipherable.

Since f~(ba)=10=f~(c)\tilde{f}(ba)=10=\tilde{f}(c)f~​(ba)=10=f~​(c)

Irreducible codes

A code f:A→S(B)f:A\to S(B)f:A→S(B) is called irreducible if for any x,y∈Ax,y\in Ax,y∈A, f(y)≠f(x)wf(y)\neq f(x)wf(y)=f(x)w for some w∈S(B)w\in S(B)w∈S(B).

This condition ensures that every message used in the encoding process is uniquely decipherable.

Means that there is no ambiguity in the decoding process.

Theorem 1.1.1 Sardinas and Patterson Theorem

Let A,BA,BA,B be alphabet sets of size a,ba,ba,b respectively.

Let f:A→S(B)f:A\to S(B)f:A→S(B) be a code that is uniquely decipherable.

Then

∑x∈Ab−l(f(x))≤1\sum_{x\in A}b^{-l(f(x))}\leq 1x∈A∑​b−l(f(x))≤1

where l(f(x))l(f(x))l(f(x)) is the length of the codeword f(x)f(x)f(x). (Number of elements used in the codeword for xxx)

Proof:

Let LLL denote the max length of the codeword for any x∈Ax\in Ax∈A.

L=max⁡{l(f(x))∣x∈A}L=\max\{l(f(x))|x\in A\}L=max{l(f(x))∣x∈A}

Let crc_rcr​ be the number of codewords of length rrr.

Then

∑x∈Ab−l(f(x))=∑r=1L∑l(f(x))=rb−l(f(x))=∑r=1Lcrb−r\begin{aligned} \sum_{x\in A}b^{-l(f(x))}&=\sum_{r=1}^{L}\sum_{l(f(x))=r}b^{-l(f(x))}\\ &=\sum_{r=1}^{L}c_rb^{-r} \end{aligned}x∈A∑​b−l(f(x))​=r=1∑L​l(f(x))=r∑​b−l(f(x))=r=1∑L​cr​b−r​

Note that rrr is the length of the codeword.

The max number of elements that can be represented by rrr elements in BBB is brb^rbr, so cr≤brc_r\leq b^rcr​≤br.

This gives that the power series

∑r=1∞b−rcr\sum_{r=1}^{\infty}b^{-r}c_rr=1∑∞​b−rcr​

converges to R=1lim sup⁡r→∞crr≤1R=\frac{1}{\limsup_{r\to\infty}\sqrt[r]{c_r}}\leq 1R=limsupr→∞​rcr​​1​≤1.

So the sum of the probabilities of all the codewords must be less than or equal to 1.

Sardinas and Patterson Algorithm

Let A={a1,a2,⋯ ,an}A=\{a_1,a_2,\cdots,a_n\}A={a1​,a2​,⋯,an​} be the message alphabet and B={b1,b2,⋯ ,bm}B=\{b_1,b_2,\cdots,b_m\}B={b1​,b2​,⋯,bm​} be the encoded alphabet.

We test whether the code f:A→S(B)f:A\to S(B)f:A→S(B) is uniquely decipherable.

def is_uniquely_decipherable(f):
    contain_common_prefix = 
    message_space=set()
    for x in A:
        if f(x) in message_space:
            return False
        message_space.add(f(x))
        for i in range(1,len(f(x))):
            if f(x)[:i] in message_space:
                contain_common_prefix = True
 
    while contain_common_prefix:
        contain_common_prefix = False
        for x in message_space:
            for y in message_space:
                code_length = min(len(x),len(y))
                if x[:code_length] == y[:code_length]:
                    contain_common_prefix = True
                    if len(x) < len(y):
                        message_space.add(y[code_length:])
                    else:
                        message_space.add(x[code_length:])
                    break
    return True

Shannon’s source coding theorem

Definition 1.1.4

An elementary information source is a pair (A,μ)(A,\mu)(A,μ) where AAA is an alphabet and μ\muμ is a probability distribution on AAA. μ\muμ is a function μ:A→[0,1]\mu:A\to[0,1]μ:A→[0,1] such that ∑a∈Aμ(a)=1\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)=1∑a∈A​μ(a)=1.

The mean code word length of an information source (A,μ)(A,\mu)(A,μ) given a code f:A→S(B)f:A\to S(B)f:A→S(B) is defined as

l‾(μ,f)=∑a∈Aμ(a)l(f(a))\overline{l}(\mu,f)=\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)l(f(a))l(μ,f)=a∈A∑​μ(a)l(f(a))

The L(μ)L(\mu)L(μ) of the mean code word length is defined as

L(μ)=min⁡{l‾(μ,f)∣f:A→S(B) is uniquely decipherable}L(\mu)=\min\{\overline{l}(\mu,f)|f:A\to S(B)\text{ is uniquely decipherable}\}L(μ)=min{l(μ,f)∣f:A→S(B) is uniquely decipherable}

Lemma: Jenson’s inequality

Let fff be a convex function on the interval (a,b)(a,b)(a,b). Then for any x1,x2,⋯ ,xn∈(a,b)x_1,x_2,\cdots,x_n\in (a,b)x1​,x2​,⋯,xn​∈(a,b) and λ1,λ2,⋯ ,λn∈[0,1]\lambda_1,\lambda_2,\cdots,\lambda_n\in [0,1]λ1​,λ2​,⋯,λn​∈[0,1] such that ∑i=1nλi=1\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i=1∑i=1n​λi​=1, we have

f(∑i=1nλixi)≤∑i=1nλif(xi)f(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_ix_i)\leq \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_if(x_i)f(∑i=1n​λi​xi​)≤∑i=1n​λi​f(xi​)

Proof:

If fff is a convex function, there are three properties that useful for the proof:

  1. f′′(x)≥0f''(x)\geq 0f′′(x)≥0 for all x∈(a,b)x\in (a,b)x∈(a,b)
  2. For any x,y∈(a,b)x,y\in (a,b)x,y∈(a,b), f(x)≥f(y)+(x−y)f′(y)f(x)\geq f(y)+(x-y)f'(y)f(x)≥f(y)+(x−y)f′(y) (Take tangent line at yyy)
  3. For any x,y∈(a,b)x,y\in (a,b)x,y∈(a,b) and 0<λ<10<\lambda<10<λ<1, we have f(λx+(1−λ)y)≤λf(x)+(1−λ)f(y)f(\lambda x+(1-\lambda)y)\leq \lambda f(x)+(1-\lambda)f(y)f(λx+(1−λ)y)≤λf(x)+(1−λ)f(y) (Take line connecting f(x)f(x)f(x) and f(y)f(y)f(y))

We use f(x)≥f(y)+(x−y)f′(y)f(x)\geq f(y)+(x-y)f'(y)f(x)≥f(y)+(x−y)f′(y), we replace y=∑i=1nλixiy=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_ix_iy=∑i=1n​λi​xi​ and x=xjx=x_jx=xj​, we have

f(xj)≥f(∑i=1nλixi)+(xj−∑i=1nλixi)f′(∑i=1nλixi)f(x_j)\geq f(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_ix_i)+(x_j-\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_ix_i)f'(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_ix_i)f(xj​)≥f(∑i=1n​λi​xi​)+(xj​−∑i=1n​λi​xi​)f′(∑i=1n​λi​xi​)

We sum all the inequalities, we have

∑j=1nλjf(xj)≥∑j=1nλjf(∑i=1nλixi)+∑j=1nλj(xj−∑i=1nλixi)f′(∑i=1nλixi)≥∑j=1nλjf(∑i=1nλixi)+0=f(∑j=1nλixj)\begin{aligned} \sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_j f(x_j)&\geq \sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_jf(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_ix_i)+\sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_j(x_j-\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_ix_i)f'(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_ix_i)\\ &\geq \sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_jf(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_ix_i)+0\\ &=f(\sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_ix_j) \end{aligned}j=1∑n​λj​f(xj​)​≥j=1∑n​λj​f(i=1∑n​λi​xi​)+j=1∑n​λj​(xj​−i=1∑n​λi​xi​)f′(i=1∑n​λi​xi​)≥j=1∑n​λj​f(i=1∑n​λi​xi​)+0=f(j=1∑n​λi​xj​)​

Theorem 1.1.5

Shannon’s source coding theorem

Let (A,μ)(A,\mu)(A,μ) be an elementary information source and let bbb be the size of the encoded alphabet BBB.

Then

−∑x∈Aμ(x)log⁡μ(x)log⁡b≤L(μ)<−∑x∈Aμ(x)log⁡μ(x)log⁡b+1\frac{-\sum_{x\in A}\mu(x)\log\mu(x)}{\log b}\leq L(\mu)<\frac{-\sum_{x\in A}\mu(x)\log\mu(x)}{\log b}+1logb−∑x∈A​μ(x)logμ(x)​≤L(μ)<logb−∑x∈A​μ(x)logμ(x)​+1

where L(μ)L(\mu)L(μ) is the minimum mean code word length of all uniquely decipherable codes for (A,μ)(A,\mu)(A,μ).

Proof

First, we show that

∑a∈Am(a)μ(a)≥−(log⁡b)−1∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡μ(a)\sum_{a\in A}m(a)\mu(a)\geq -(\log b)^{-1}\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log \mu(a)a∈A∑​m(a)μ(a)≥−(logb)−1a∈A∑​μ(a)logμ(a)

Let m:A→Z+m:A\to \mathbb{Z}_+m:A→Z+​ be a map satisfying

∑a∈Ab−m(a)≤1\sum_{a\in A}b^{-m(a)}\leq 1a∈A∑​b−m(a)≤1

We defined the probability distribution vvv on AAA as

v(x)=b−m(x)∑a∈Ab−m(a)=b−m(x)Tv(x)=\frac{b^{-m(x)}}{\sum_{a\in A}b^{-m(a)}}=\frac{b^{-m(x)}}{T}v(x)=∑a∈A​b−m(a)b−m(x)​=Tb−m(x)​

Write T=∑a∈Ab−m(a)T=\sum_{a\in A}b^{-m(a)}T=∑a∈A​b−m(a) so that T≤1T\leq 1T≤1.

Since b−m(a)=T⋅v(a)b^{-m(a)}=T\cdot v(a)b−m(a)=T⋅v(a),

−m(a)log⁡b=log⁡T+log⁡v(a)-m(a)\log b=\log T+\log v(a)−m(a)logb=logT+logv(a), m(a)=−log⁡Tlog⁡b−log⁡v(a)log⁡bm(a)=-\frac{\log T}{\log b}-\frac{\log v(a)}{\log b}m(a)=−logblogT​−logblogv(a)​

We have

∑a∈Am(a)μ(a)=∑a∈A(−log⁡Tlog⁡b−log⁡v(a)log⁡b)μ(a)=−log⁡Tlog⁡b∑a∈Aμ(a)−1log⁡b∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡v(a)=(log⁡b)−1{−log⁡T−∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡v(a)}\begin{aligned} \sum_{a\in A}m(a)\mu(a)&=\sum_{a\in A}\left(-\frac{\log T}{\log b}-\frac{\log v(a)}{\log b}\right)\mu(a)\\ &=-\frac{\log T}{\log b}\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)-\frac{1}{\log b}\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log v(a)\\ &=(\log b)^{-1}\left\{-\log T - \sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log v(a)\right\} \end{aligned}a∈A∑​m(a)μ(a)​=a∈A∑​(−logblogT​−logblogv(a)​)μ(a)=−logblogT​a∈A∑​μ(a)−logb1​a∈A∑​μ(a)logv(a)=(logb)−1{−logT−a∈A∑​μ(a)logv(a)}​

Without loss of generality, we assume that μ(a)>0\mu(a)>0μ(a)>0 for all a∈Aa\in Aa∈A.

−∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡v(a)=−∑a∈Aμ(a)(log⁡μ(a)+log⁡v(a)−log⁡μ(a))=−∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡μ(a)−∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡v(a)μ(a)=−∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡μ(a)−log⁡∏a∈A(v(a)μ(a))μ(a)\begin{aligned} -\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log v(a)&=-\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)(\log \mu(a)+\log v(a)-\log \mu(a))\\ &=-\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log \mu(a)-\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log \frac{v(a)}{\mu(a)}\\ &=-\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log \mu(a)-\log \prod_{a\in A}\left(\frac{v(a)}{\mu(a)}\right)^{\mu(a)} \end{aligned}−a∈A∑​μ(a)logv(a)​=−a∈A∑​μ(a)(logμ(a)+logv(a)−logμ(a))=−a∈A∑​μ(a)logμ(a)−a∈A∑​μ(a)logμ(a)v(a)​=−a∈A∑​μ(a)logμ(a)−loga∈A∏​(μ(a)v(a)​)μ(a)​

log⁡∏a∈A(v(a)μ(a))μ(a)=∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡v(a)μ(a)\log \prod_{a\in A}\left(\frac{v(a)}{\mu(a)}\right)^{\mu(a)}=\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log \frac{v(a)}{\mu(a)}log∏a∈A​(μ(a)v(a)​)μ(a)=∑a∈A​μ(a)logμ(a)v(a)​ is also called Kullback–Leibler divergence or relative entropy.

Since log⁡\loglog is a concave function, by Jensen’s inequality f(∑i=1nλixi)≤∑i=1nλif(xi)f(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_ix_i)\leq \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_if(x_i)f(∑i=1n​λi​xi​)≤∑i=1n​λi​f(xi​), we have

∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡v(a)μ(a)≤log⁡(∑a∈Aμ(a)v(a)μ(a))log⁡(∏a∈A(v(a)μ(a))μ(a))≤log⁡(∑a∈Aμ(a)v(a)μ(a))∏a∈A(v(a)μ(a))μ(a)≤∑a∈Aμ(a)v(a)μ(a)=1\begin{aligned} \sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log \frac{v(a)}{\mu(a)}&\leq\log\left(\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a) \frac{v(a)}{\mu(a)}\right)\\ \log\left(\prod_{a\in A}\left(\frac{v(a)}{\mu(a)}\right)^{\mu(a)}\right)&\leq \log\left(\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a) \frac{v(a)}{\mu(a)}\right)\\ \prod_{a\in A}\left(\frac{v(a)}{\mu(a)}\right)^{\mu(a)}&\leq \sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\frac{v(a)}{\mu(a)}=1 \end{aligned}a∈A∑​μ(a)logμ(a)v(a)​log(a∈A∏​(μ(a)v(a)​)μ(a))a∈A∏​(μ(a)v(a)​)μ(a)​≤log(a∈A∑​μ(a)μ(a)v(a)​)≤log(a∈A∑​μ(a)μ(a)v(a)​)≤a∈A∑​μ(a)μ(a)v(a)​=1​

So

−∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡v(a)≥−∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡μ(a)-\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log v(a)\geq -\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log \mu(a)−a∈A∑​μ(a)logv(a)≥−a∈A∑​μ(a)logμ(a)

(This is also known as Gibbs’ inequality: Put in words, the information entropy of a distribution PPP is less than or equal to its cross entropy with any other distribution QQQ.)

Since T≤1T\leq 1T≤1, −log⁡T≥0-\log T\geq 0−logT≥0, we have

∑a∈Am(a)μ(a)≥−(log⁡b)−1∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡μ(a)\sum_{a\in A}m(a)\mu(a)\geq -(\log b)^{-1}\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log \mu(a)a∈A∑​m(a)μ(a)≥−(logb)−1a∈A∑​μ(a)logμ(a)

Second, we show that

∑a∈Am(a)μ(a)≤−(log⁡b)−1∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡μ(a)+1\sum_{a\in A}m(a)\mu(a)\leq -(\log b)^{-1}\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log \mu(a)+1a∈A∑​m(a)μ(a)≤−(logb)−1a∈A∑​μ(a)logμ(a)+1

By Theorem 1.1.1, there exists an irreducible code f:A→S(B)f:A\to S(B)f:A→S(B) such that l(f(a))=m(a)l(f(a))=m(a)l(f(a))=m(a) for all a∈Aa\in Aa∈A.

So

l‾(μ,f)=∑a∈Aμ(a)m(a)<∑a∈Aμ(a)(1−log⁡μ(a)log⁡b)=−∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡μ(a)⋅1log⁡b+1=−(log⁡b)−1∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡μ(a)+1\begin{aligned} \overline{l}(\mu,f)&=\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)m(a)\\ &<\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\left(1-\frac{\log\mu(a)}{\log b}\right)\\ &=-\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log\mu(a)\cdot\frac{1}{\log b}+1\\ &=-(\log b)^{-1}\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log \mu(a)+1 \end{aligned}l(μ,f)​=a∈A∑​μ(a)m(a)<a∈A∑​μ(a)(1−logblogμ(a)​)=−a∈A∑​μ(a)logμ(a)⋅logb1​+1=−(logb)−1a∈A∑​μ(a)logμ(a)+1​

Entropy

Let AAA be an alphabet and let P(A)\mathcal{P}(A)P(A) be the set of all probability distributions on AAA. Any element μ∈P(A)\mu\in \mathcal{P}(A)μ∈P(A) is thus a map from AAA to [0,1][0,1][0,1] such that ∑a∈Aμ(a)=1\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)=1∑a∈A​μ(a)=1.

Thus P(A)\mathcal{P}(A)P(A) is a compact conves subset of real linear space RA\mathbb{R}^ARA.

We define the map H:P(A)→RH:\mathcal{P}(A)\to\mathbb{R}H:P(A)→R as

H(μ)=−∑a∈Aμ(a)log⁡2μ(a)H(\mu)=-\sum_{a\in A}\mu(a)\log_2\mu(a)H(μ)=−a∈A∑​μ(a)log2​μ(a)

Basic properties of HHH

  1. H(μ)≥0H(\mu)\geq 0H(μ)≥0
  2. H(μ)=0H(\mu)=0H(μ)=0 if and only if μ\muμ is a point mass.
  3. H(μ)=log⁡2∣A∣H(\mu)=\log_2|A|H(μ)=log2​∣A∣ if and only if μ\muμ is uniform.

Huffman’s coding algorithm

Huffman’s coding algorithm is a greedy algorithm that constructs an optimal prefix code for a given probability distribution.

The algorithm is as follows:

  1. Sort the symbols by their probabilities in descending order.
  2. Merge the two symbols with the smallest probabilities and assign a new codeword to the merged symbol.
  3. Repeat step 2 until there is only one symbol left.
import heapq
 
def huffman(frequencies):
    class Node:
        def __init__(self, symbol=None, freq=0, left=None, right=None):
            self.symbol = symbol
            self.freq = freq
            self.left = left
            self.right = right
        def __lt__(self, other):  # For priority queue
            return self.freq < other.freq
        def is_leaf(self):
            return self.symbol is not None
 
    # Build the Huffman tree
    heap = [Node(sym, freq) for sym, freq in frequencies.items()]
    heapq.heapify(heap)
    while len(heap) > 1:
        left = heapq.heappop(heap)
        right = heapq.heappop(heap)
        merged = Node(freq=left.freq + right.freq, left=left, right=right)
        heapq.heappush(heap, merged)
    root = heap[0]
 
    # Assign codes by traversing the tree
    codebook = {}
    def assign_codes(node, code=""):
        if node.is_leaf():
            codebook[node.symbol] = code
        else:
            assign_codes(node.left, code + "0")
            assign_codes(node.right, code + "1")
    assign_codes(root)
 
    # Helper to pretty print the binary tree
    def tree_repr(node, prefix=""):
        if node.is_leaf():
            return f"{prefix}→ {repr(node.symbol)} ({node.freq})\n"
        else:
            s = f"{prefix}• ({node.freq})\n"
            s += tree_repr(node.left, prefix + "  0 ")
            s += tree_repr(node.right, prefix + "  1 ")
            return s
 
    print("Huffman Codebook:")
    for sym in sorted(codebook, key=lambda s: frequencies[s], reverse=True):
        print(f"{repr(sym)}: {codebook[sym]}")
    print("\nCoding Tree:")
    print(tree_repr(root))
 
    return codebook

Definition 1.2.1

Let A={a1,a2,⋯ ,an}A=\{a_1,a_2,\cdots,a_n\}A={a1​,a2​,⋯,an​} be an alphabet and let μ=(μ(a1),μ(a2),⋯ ,μ(an))\mu=(\mu(a_1),\mu(a_2),\cdots,\mu(a_n))μ=(μ(a1​),μ(a2​),⋯,μ(an​)) be a probability distribution on AAA.

Proof:

We use mathematical induction on the number of symbols nnn.

Base Case: n=2n=2n=2

Only two symbols — assign one “0”, the other “1”. This is clearly optimal (only possible choice).

Inductive Step:

Assume that Huffman’s algorithm produces an optimal code for n−1n-1n−1 symbols.

Now, given nnn symbols, Huffman merges the two least probable symbols a,ba,ba,b into ccc, and constructs an optimal code recursively for n−1n-1n−1 symbols. n−1 symbols.

By the inductive hypothesis, the code on A′A'A′ is optimal. is optimal.

By Step 2 above, assigning the two merged symbols aaa and bbb codewords w0w_0w0​ and w1w_1w1​ (based on 1.1.4) results in the optimal solution for AAA.

Therefore, by induction, Huffman’s algorithm gives an optimal prefix code for any nnn.
